roots.title: Course Introduction and Pandas Overview
roots.summary: The Data 100 course covers the pandas library, focusing on DataFrames, Series, and Indices. Students will learn data extraction methods using loc and iloc, with an emphasis on understanding API documentation. The course will include exploratory data analysis (EDA) using a dataset on U.S. presidential candidates from 1824 to 2020.

children.title: Course Introduction and Pandas Overview
children.summary: The Data 100 course begins with a welcome and logistics reminder, including room capacity and fire safety. It will focus on the pandas library, covering key data structures such as DataFrames, Series, and Indices over three lectures. Students will learn data extraction methods using loc and iloc, with an emphasis on understanding API documentation and a reference sheet provided for midterm functions.

children.title: Course Introduction and Pandas Overview
children.summary: The Data 100 course starts with a welcome and logistics reminder, including room capacity and fire safety. It will focus on the pandas library, covering key data structures like DataFrames, Series, and Indices across three lectures. Students will learn data extraction methods using loc and iloc, emphasizing understanding API documentation, with a reference sheet provided for midterm functions.

children.title: Course Introduction and Pandas Overview
children.summary: The Data 100 course begins with a welcome and logistics reminder about room capacity and fire safety. The focus will be on the pandas library, essential for data science, covering key data structures like DataFrames, Series, and Indices over three lectures. Students will learn data extraction methods using loc and iloc, with an emphasis on understanding API documentation rather than memorizing syntax, and a reference sheet will be provided for midterm functions.

children.title: Course Introduction and Logistics
children.summary: The session begins with a welcome to the Data 100 course, marking the first content lecture. Students are reminded to take their seats to comply with room capacity and fire safety regulations.

children.title: Introduction to pandas library for data science
children.summary: The session will focus on pandas, a Python programming library essential for data science. It is widely used in data-related courses and is a fundamental tool for coding in Python as a data scientist.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right, cool. So the plan for today is, as I told you last time is that we're going to talk about pandas. But this pandas pandas is a programming library, Python programming library, that we're going to use a lot in data science and in data 100 and kind of every time that as a data scientist you try to code in Python you'll be using pandas. children.title: Introduction to pandas: Data structures and extraction
children.summary: Pandas will be covered in a three-lecture sequence, focusing on its key data structures including DataFrames, Series, and Indices. The lectures will also address how to load data and perform data extraction using methods like loc, iloc, and indexing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let me fix the audio. All right, cool. So what we're going to do actually today and in the next couple of lectures is to immerse you in everything pandas. So we're going to start by talking about the data structures that we have in pandas, how to load the data, what are different data structures that you can load and play with the data. And then we're going to also start talking about how we can do data extraction. If you want to filter your data set so that you look at a bunch of features and only a limited number of rows, how do we do that? Video: 1: X

children.title: Introduction to Pandas: Overview and Goals
children.summary: Pandas is introduced as a key Python library for data manipulation, focusing on essential data structures like DataFrames, Series, and Indices. The lecture will cover data extraction methods such as loc and iloc, and students should prepare for a significant amount of coding. This is the first in a series of three lectures aimed at familiarizing students with Pandas syntax, which will be reinforced through lab work and homework.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's also something that we're going to talk about today and that's using lock, eye lock and selection operator that you're seeing in the agenda. that today what we are going to go over is to introduce you with Pandas programming leveries so you can expect that there's a whole lot of code coming your way. So you can be overwhelming. We're going to keep talking about new syntax and new syntax and I'll keep throwing these new syntax at you. So time is it might be overwhelming and that's totally fine. What you are expecting from the lecture in today's lecture and in the next couple of lectures is that we're going to show you the roadmap. And you're going to get actually used to the syntax and learn more about the syntax in lab and in homeworks. Video: 1: v

children.title: Using API Documentation for Pandas
children.summary: The focus is on utilizing API documentation to understand available functionalities in pandas for labs and homework, emphasizing that memorization of syntax is unnecessary. Students are encouraged to grasp the underlying logic of pandas, with the assurance that they will become familiar with syntax through practice. A comprehensive reference sheet will be provided for midterm functions, aiding in their understanding and application.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we're going to talk about today, and also again in the next couple of lectures, is how to use the API documentation in order to figure out what functionality is out there so that you can use those online resources as you're working on your lab and your homework. So the actual deep learning that's going to happen in pandas is going to happen in lab and homework. So don't expect to memorize everything. We don't expect you to memorize all the syntax of recovering today. So only just listen to the logic, because pandas, the way that it's implemented and all the syntaxes that you just need to make sense of the logic, and then everything else will become, you'll become used to it as you keep using it. And I think that's about It in terms of um What you're gonna see today oh the last thing that I wanted to say again the reason that you don't need to memorize this stuff is that as you're working on the labs and homework so you have the pandas a p i documentation so you can go ahead and read all of those documentation see a bunch of examples you'll be able to replicate it for me turn on final example nice thing about data 100 is that we give you a very comprehensive reference sheet so all of the functions that you'll be using in the midterm that the brief description of that is going to be on that reference view. children.title: Data Science Lifecycle: EDA and Tabular Structure
children.summary: The data science lifecycle highlights the significance of understanding data, utilizing tools like pandas for processing and visualization. Analysis can begin with a question or existing data, with a focus on exploratory data analysis (EDA) for the first three weeks, using a dataset on U.S. presidential candidates from 1824 to 2020, which includes 182 individuals and six features such as names and party affiliations.

children.title: Data Science Lifecycle: EDA and Tabular Structure
children.summary: The data science lifecycle emphasizes the importance of understanding data, with tools like pandas for processing and visualization. Two entry points for analysis are identified: starting with a question or with data. The course will focus on exploratory data analysis (EDA) over the first three weeks, covering data loading, processing, and visualization using a dataset on U.S. presidential candidates from 1824 to 2020, which includes attributes like names and party affiliations. In tabular data, each row represents an observation and columns represent attributes, with the dataset containing 182 individuals and six features.

children.title: Data Science Lifecycle: Entry Points and EDA
children.summary: The importance of understanding data in the data science lifecycle is discussed, emphasizing tools like pandas for data processing and visualization. Two entry points are identified: starting with a question or beginning with data provision. The focus for the upcoming weeks will be on exploratory data analysis (EDA).

children.title: Data Science Lifecycle: Data Entry Points and EDA
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of understanding data within the data science lifecycle, highlighting the need for tools like pandas to process and visualize data. It outlines two entry points into the lifecycle: starting with a question or beginning with data provision. The emphasis for the upcoming weeks will be on exploratory data analysis (EDA).

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We say that, okay, we're dealing with the data, were extracting the data, we're processing the data, but we didn't talk about what this data is. So talking about the data and where data comes in this data science lifecycle that we saw in the previous lecture, we've been over a great demo with a lot of different pandas and text. And you saw that we need pandas in order to be able to process loads of data and be able to do fun things, whether it be able to visualize the data, to filter the data and extract information from the data that we have. We saw that there are two entry points into this data science lifecycle. There's one thing that we ask a question. And then we go about finding the data or that the lifecycle starts with providing the data to you. What we're going to expect, I spent a lot of time in the first three weeks is the exploratory data analysis that we're, for short, we're calling it EDA. Video: 1: v

children.title: Introduction to EDA: Data Loading and Tabular Structure
children.summary: The first three weeks of Data 100 will focus on Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), including data loading, processing, visualization, and textual data handling. Understanding the structure of tabular data, which resembles a spreadsheet, is crucial for data science. The course will utilize a specific elections dataset detailing U.S. presidential candidates, including names, party affiliations, popular vote results, and election years from 1824 to 2020.

children.title: Introduction to EDA: Data Loading and Processing
children.summary: The focus of the first three weeks of Data 100 will be on Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), covering data loading, processing, visualization, and handling textual information. The instructor emphasizes the importance of understanding the data being used, beyond just the abstract concept of data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in EDA, we're going to show you how you can load the data, how to process the data, how to visualize the data, how to process textual information, and also in addition to that, how to be able to visualize all the information that you have in the data set that you have. So this is the entire kind of, we're gonna spend the first three weeks of data 100 so that you know where we stand. But I basically talked about data a lot. I say that we're giving you the data, we're handing you the data, we are processing the data, but I don't talk about what this data is, other than this abstract idea of me throwing a box of data at you. children.title: Understanding Tabular Data Structure
children.summary: Tabular data is structured in a table format, similar to a spreadsheet, and is central to data science workflows. The lecture will focus on a specific elections dataset containing information about U.S. presidential candidates, including their names, party affiliations, popular vote results, and election years, spanning from 1824 to 2020.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So today, we're going to talk about what this data actually is, how is it stored, how can we load and process data in different formats, and what is the standard way for us to think about data? So we're going to think about the data as a tabular data. in in data 100 and in generally when we are talking about data science workflow. So what is a tabular data tabular data means that it a data that lives in a table. So what you're seeing in this example is that I have a table which is like a panel or a spreadsheet like of the data. And this data is the data set that will work with in today's lecture and in the next couple of lectures to come. So this is elections data set. So assume that someone handed me the data that was collected about all the individuals who ran for the presidents of the United States, their names, their party affiliation, the popular vote that they got, the result and the percentage of vote that they got in that specific election as well as the year. So you can see that it starts from 1824 and then goes all the way to the 2020 election. So this is the data set that we're working with. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: So today, we're going to talk about what this data actually is, how is it stored, how can we load and process data in different formats, and what is the standard way for us to think about data? So we're going to think about the data as a tabular data. in in data 100 and in generally when we are talking about data science workflow. So what is a tabular data tabular data means that it a data that lives in a table. So what you're seeing in this example is that I have a table which is like a panel or a spreadsheet like of the data. And this data is the data set that will work with in today's lecture and in the next couple of lectures to come. So this is elections data set. So assume that someone handed me the data that was collected about all the individuals who ran for the presidents of the United States, their names, their party affiliation, the popular vote that they got, the result and the percentage of vote that they got in that specific election as well as the year. So you can see that it starts from 1824 and then goes all the way to the 2020 election. So this is the data set that we're working with.

children.title: Understanding Tabular Data Structure and Features
children.summary: In tabular data, each row represents a single observation while columns represent attributes of those observations. The dataset consists of 182 individuals characterized by six features, including year, candidate name, and party affiliation, structured in a 2D format.

children.title: Understanding Rows and Columns in Tabular Data
children.summary: In tabular data, each row represents a single observation or sample, while columns represent attributes or features of those observations. For example, a row might correspond to an individual like John Adams, with features such as party affiliation. The discussion will also cover handling missing values in future assignments.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But as data scientists, we have a contract that when we're thinking about a tabular data in 2D format, we're always referring to rows as a single observation or a single sample or a single individual in this data set that we had dealt with. So for example, in this case, this single row corresponds to John Adams, who ran for presidents in 1824 or the time that the 1824 year that they ran for presidents, if there's someone who ran multiple times or over multiple different years. So we think about Rose as each one of those individual observations or a sample, we're going to keep talking about the resolution of the or the granularity of our data set in features lectures, but for today you just assume that whatever the granularity or the resolution of your data is, you're going to refer to one single data point as one of the rows in your data. And obviously the columns are going to be attributes or different features of those of those data points that you're holding. So for example here, this is the feature related to party. So all of those individuals do have party affiliation or if there is data missing you will see that that feature is no That's something that you will see in homeworks and labs and we're gonna take a look at that later on and let's see how we can handle Missing values, but for this is specific case all of the data The entire data set is filled in with all of the possible values And then what you're seeing is that party is a feature or is an attribute? children.title: Dataset Features and Structure Overview
children.summary: The dataset contains 182 individuals, each characterized by six features, including year, candidate name, and party affiliation. The structure is represented in a 2D format with rows for samples and columns for features, akin to a table in data aid.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're going to talk about feature, attribute, or characteristic of those individuals. So what are different features that we have in this data set? We have a feature for a year, we have a feature for the candidate name, we have a feature for party affiliation and so on and so forth. So you can see that we have a data set with x number of individuals. So how many individuals or how many samples we have? We have 182 right because we have those labels also listed here labels starting from zero one and then it goes all the way to 181 that shows me that if this sequence of numbers is sequential then I have 182 different individuals or samples in my data set and then how many features I have I basically have feature 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. So I have six features in my dataset. So this is how we can think about a dataset in 2D format, and then we have rows and we have columns, and that's how we are going to always refer to samples and features in our dataset. leaf_chunk.audio: So we're going to talk about feature, attribute, or characteristic of those individuals. So what are different features that we have in this data set? We have a feature for a year, we have a feature for the candidate name, we have a feature for party affiliation and so on and so forth. So you can see that we have a data set with x number of individuals. So how many individuals or how many samples we have? We have 182 right because we have those labels also listed here labels starting from zero one and then it goes all the way to 181 that shows me that if this sequence of numbers is sequential then I have 182 different individuals or samples in my data set and then how many features I have I basically have feature 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. So I have six features in my dataset. So this is how we can think about a dataset in 2D format, and then we have rows and we have columns, and that's how we are going to always refer to samples and features in our dataset. So in data aid, those who are coming from data aid, you have seen that this concept of a table was represented using the tables class.

children.title: Introduction to Pandas: DataFrames and Series
children.summary: Pandas is a programming library for handling panel data, with DataFrames as the main 2D structure for organizing tabular data. It facilitates data extraction, visualization, and filtering, important for tasks like analyzing election vote percentages. Feature engineering enhances data for modeling using Numpy's efficient vectorized operations. A Pandas Series is a one-dimensional structure with labeled values and an index, created from lists.

children.title: Introduction to Pandas: DataFrames and Series
children.summary: Pandas is a programming library essential for handling panel data, with DataFrames as a primary 2D structure for organizing data in tabular form. It supports data extraction, visualization, and filtering, crucial for tasks like analyzing election vote percentages. Feature engineering transforms data for modeling, leveraging Numpy's vectorized operations for efficiency. Resources on the course website include code examples and mobile-friendly notebooks, while a Pandas Series is a one-dimensional structure with labeled values, created from lists and featuring an index for identification.

children.title: Introduction to Pandas: DataFrames and Feature Engineering
children.summary: Pandas is a programming library for handling panel data, with DataFrames as a key 2D structure for organizing data in a tabular format. It enables data extraction, visualization, and filtering for insights, such as analyzing election vote percentages. Feature engineering is crucial for transforming data into useful forms for modeling, utilizing vectorized operations from Numpy to enhance efficiency in calculations.

children.title: Introduction to Pandas and DataFrames
children.summary: Pandas is a programming library designed for handling panel data, originally developed for finance. In Data 100, a data frame is introduced as a key 2D data structure supported by pandas, which allows for organizing data in a tabular format. The lecture emphasizes the functionality of pandas, including data loading and viewing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you were loading the data into tables. In data 100, we're going to refer to a 2D data structure like this as a data frame. And we're going to talk about what data frame is. And that's your introduction to the first data structure that you're seeing that is supported by the pandas library that we have. I kept talking about pandas pandas what is pandas pandas stands for panel data and the term came from finance where they had panels of data or transaction data that they had to load and process and then they wrote the preliminary kind of library that then got adopted by the CS community and got extended over. So this panda stands for panel data. It doesn't have anything to do with panda bear, but because in data 100, we love cute animals. Then we design all of the logos in data 100 around having a panda kind of flying somewhere in some of our logos. And you'll keep seeing that in handouts and in different places. And you're also seeing that on the website as well. So let's get into what pandas can do. So I say pandas is a programming library. So it's a programming library that gives you a lot of functionality. And starting functionality that we want is basically to be able to arrange data in a tab alert format. There are different data structures that are supported by pandas, how we can load the data, how we can take a look at the data. Video: 1: v

children.title: Data Extraction in Pandas: Insights and Visualization
children.summary: Pandas functionality allows for extracting useful information from datasets, such as determining the maximum percentage of votes a candidate received in an election. Users can gain insights, visualize data, and apply filters to process information effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It will tell us how we can extract useful information from the data. So, for example, if you have this election dataset, and if you want to extract, for example, what is the maximum popular percentage of votes that a candidate got? You can basically use Pandas functionality in order to search through your data frame and be able to extract that information. You can gain insights, you can visualize it, you can filter it, you can do all fancy stuff in order to process your data and gain more information. Video: 1: v

children.title: Feature Engineering for Modeling Tasks
children.summary: Feature engineering involves transforming features, such as popular percentage, into more useful forms for modeling. This is part of the third stage of the data science lifecycle, with the fourth stage focused on modeling, emphasizing the importance of preparing data to facilitate the modeling process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You can do feature engineering. So for example, say that you have the popular percentage, right? If you want to change that feature into a different feature that is more useful for the modeling tasks that you will do down the road, remember that we're in the third data science lifecycle, the fourth data science lifecycle stage is going to be modeling. So we'll keep doing everything possible to do the modeling to make the modeling phase as easy as possible. Video: 1: v

children.title: Feature Engineering and Vectorized Operations
children.summary: Feature engineering transforms data, such as converting percentages into ratios based on the highest vote percentage in an election. Numpy facilitates these transformations by allowing vectorized operations, enabling efficient calculations like vector multiplication and addition, which streamline model training and processing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we might want to do feature engineering. Feature engineering is that, okay, I have these all of these percentages, but what if I want to turn those percentages into ratio, ratio over like the, the highest percentage of votes that it can't get at God in that specific election right that's an example of transforming the data uh... in terms of doing transformation there are a lot of times that we can use numpy so we're using numpy so that we're not implementing all of those uh... mathematical calculations ourselves and another Kind of benefit of pandas is that it will perform all of those operations in a vectorized way. So remember that we had columns as features so it will turn one specific column into one long vector or one very large vector. And then all of the calculation that we're doing is going to be vector calculation, vector multiplication, vector addition and different different kind of operations that we run on vectors. so that the processing can be paralyzed and then the calculation or training of models can be easier. children.title: Introduction to Pandas and Course Resources
children.summary: Pandas is established as the standard for data science, offering extensive functionalities for data transformation into accessible formats. Learning Pandas is compared to language translation, with resources available on the course website, including code examples and mobile-friendly notebooks. Students are encouraged to utilize a data folder with resources and a data aid translation to facilitate their learning.

children.title: Introduction to Pandas for Data Science
children.summary: Pandas is highlighted as the industry and academic standard for data science operations, providing a superset of functionalities compared to other data science libraries. The lecture emphasizes the importance of transforming data into tables or formats that facilitate easier computation and understanding of data representation.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Introduction to Pandas Data Frames and Series
children.summary: Data frames in Pandas represent tabular structures with labeled rows and columns, facilitating data manipulation. Each column in a DataFrame is a Series object, a one-dimensional array-like structure identified by a name and containing values. For instance, a 'candidate' series consists of 182 elements, with its data type classified as an object, displaying values with labels on the left.

children.title: Introduction to Data Frames in Pandas
children.summary: Data frames in pandas represent tabular data structures consisting of rows and columns. Each row is labeled, which is highlighted in a bold color or letters, facilitating data identification and manipulation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So. Now let's dive into starting to know what are those data structures? How can we create them? How can we use them? I'm going to start with data frame because we said that tables are data frames in pandas language. And this table is this this to the structure. It has a bunch of columns. It has a bunch of rows. One thing that I want you to note is that each one of those rows they have a label that is kind of noted by a bold color or both letters. Video: 1: v

children.title: Pandas Data Structures: Series and DataFrames
children.summary: Columns in a DataFrame serve as labels for attributes, forming a 2D structure composed of multiple columns. Each column is represented as a Series object, which is a 1D data structure in pandas.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then we also have labels for the columns, or these are the names for the columns, that are also labels that are also showing what attribute or what's the name of that attribute in each one of those respective columns. The way that I want you to think about this 2D structure is that it is a collection of columns. So we have a bunch of columns that we aggregate it together in order to build a data structure called data frame. and each one of those columns is a series object. series is another data structure in pandas. So series is a one D structure. Video: 1: v

children.title: Series Object: Definition and Characteristics
children.summary: A series object is a one-dimensional array-like structure in Pandas, identified by a name and containing values. For example, a 'candidate' series has 182 elements, with its data type recognized as an object. Unlike data frames, which present data in a table format, series display values with labels on the left side, and additional information about length and data type is provided.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's a sequence of values. So you can think about, for example, if I look at the candidate, candidate by itself, that single column is a series object. It has a name and the name is actually this candidate. So you're seeing that if I go ahead and print the value in that specific column, it will say that the name of this series object is candidate. It will tell me that the lengths of this candidate, the candidate series object is 182. We established that because we have 182 different elements in that specific column. And then it also says that the data type is object because it's string able to mathematically convert that and recognizes that as as objects for us. What you're seeing is that the format of a serious object is a slightly different than the data frame. Data frame is this nicely looking to the table like a structure versus every time that you're printing a serious object. The serious object has a bunch of values. It has all of those labels also tagging on kind of the left-hand side as a kind of a secondary column, but it's not a column. It's just labels for each one of those values that we have. And then at the bottom it says that the name of the series was whatever, the lengths was whatever, and the objects, the type of the data that this series object holds is, for example, integer or object and whatnot. So these are the two structures that we are going to see. And there is a third one that we're going to take a look at that afterwards. But before I jump into data frame, because we know series is the building block of a data frame, I want to go ahead and start things by just one of these structures that's the simplest of all. So a series object, as I said, is a one-dimensional array like object. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: It's a sequence of values. So you can think about, for example, if I look at the candidate, candidate by itself, that single column is a series object. It has a name and the name is actually this candidate. So you're seeing that if I go ahead and print the value in that specific column, it will say that the name of this series object is candidate. It will tell me that the lengths of this candidate, the candidate series object is 182. We established that because we have 182 different elements in that specific column. And then it also says that the data type is object because it's string able to mathematically convert that and recognizes that as as objects for us. What you're seeing is that the format of a serious object is a slightly different than the data frame. Data frame is this nicely looking to the table like a structure versus every time that you're printing a serious object. The serious object has a bunch of values. It has all of those labels also tagging on kind of the left-hand side as a kind of a secondary column, but it's not a column. It's just labels for each one of those values that we have. And then at the bottom it says that the name of the series was whatever, the lengths was whatever, and the objects, the type of the data that this series object holds is, for example, integer or object and whatnot. So these are the two structures that we are going to see. And there is a third one that we're going to take a look at that afterwards. But before I jump into data frame, because we know series is the building block of a data frame, I want to go ahead and start things by just one of these structures that's the simplest of all. So a series object, as I said, is a one-dimensional array like object.

children.title: Creating and Understanding Pandas Series
children.summary: A Pandas Series is a one-dimensional structure with labeled values, created by importing the pandas library. Series can be constructed from a list of values, where each value is associated with a label for identification. The series consists of an index, which labels the values, and the actual values themselves, with the index being a separate manipulable data structure.

children.title: Creating a Pandas Series Object
children.summary: A series is defined as a one-dimensional structure with a single column of values, annotated by labels. To create a series object, the pandas library must be imported using 'import pandas as pd'.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why do I say it's one-dimensional? Because I have one column of values, one set of values. This second set of values that you're seeing on the left, that is here 0, 1, 2. These are just labels that are annotating that, OK, at label 0 or at location 0, I have value welcome. At location 1 or at label 1, I have value 2. And at location 2, I have value data 100. But the structure we're considering series as a one-vious structure because there is one column of values that we're only concerned with. How do I create a series object? First of all, what I need to do is I need to import pandas. We import pandas as PD and views. PD is the earliest for pandas. Video: 1: v

children.title: Pandas: Creating Series with Labels
children.summary: Pandas allows the creation of a Series object by providing a list of values, which can include multiple string entries. Each value in the Series must be associated with a label or key for proper identification, even if labels are not explicitly provided.

children.title: Series and Index Objects in Data Structures
children.summary: A series object consists of two components: an index and the actual values stored. The index labels the values, while the values are accessible through the series object. Additionally, the index is a separate data structure that can be created and manipulated, with examples showing how to set and retrieve index values and how series objects relate to data frames.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It automatically created all of these values 0, 1, 2 automatically labeling the first one as 0, the second one as 1, and the third one as 2. So a series object has two parts as you can imagine. There is one part that is, that's all of these labels that we're referring to that as the index, uh, value index that, uh, values. And then there is the actual values that are restored. Um, so therefore we can go ahead and get the values, um, that are restored in the series object. We could say that, um, if I create that this S object or S is a series object, I can say S that values, And it will tell me that okay, the values that you stored are in an array with all of these values, and it will give me, will volunteer the information about the data type. And again, because there's string that recognizes object. The second piece of information that we have is all of these. Let me remove this. All of these labels that are stored in an index object. So this is the third data structure that we have. We talked about data frame that is a collection of serious objects. Each one of the serious objects is representing one column. And one serious object has its own values. And then it has the index tack to those values. So index is the third data structure that we're seeing. I'm ridden with all the data structures. leaf_chunk.audio: It automatically created all of these values 0, 1, 2 automatically labeling the first one as 0, the second one as 1, and the third one as 2. So a series object has two parts as you can imagine. There is one part that is, that's all of these labels that we're referring to that as the index, uh, value index that, uh, values. And then there is the actual values that are restored. Um, so therefore we can go ahead and get the values, um, that are restored in the series object. We could say that, um, if I create that this S object or S is a series object, I can say S that values, And it will tell me that okay, the values that you stored are in an array with all of these values, and it will give me, will volunteer the information about the data type. And again, because there's string that recognizes object. The second piece of information that we have is all of these. Let me remove this. All of these labels that are stored in an index object. So this is the third data structure that we have. We talked about data frame that is a collection of serious objects. Each one of the serious objects is representing one column. And one serious object has its own values. And then it has the index tack to those values. So index is the third data structure that we're seeing. I'm ridden with all the data structures. But from this point on, we're just going to talk about how can we create index object, how can we set index object, how can we store values in series, how can we store values in data frame, and we'll take it from there. So if I go ahead and here, for example, I say that, OK, s dot index, it will tell me that, oh, you didn't provide your index, but I went ahead and create a range object or a range index object. I started the range index at zero. I went ahead and created and tacked each one of these rows with these numerical values up to 3 and not including 3 that's how the range object works. And then it will tell me that this step. value is equal to one because it will just is that it is included by one every time.

children.title: Pandas Series and DataFrame: Creation and Indexing
children.summary: The advantages of using a Pandas Series over a DataFrame are discussed, including methods for converting Series to DataFrames and custom indexing. Creation techniques for Series and DataFrames are covered, including importing from CSV files and using dictionaries, with emphasis on alignment and filtering. The structure of DataFrames is explained, detailing the shape, index resetting, and the importance of the Pandas API for further support.

children.title: Pandas Series: Advantages, Indexing, and Filtering
children.summary: The advantages of using a Pandas Series over a DataFrame are discussed, highlighting the necessity of DataFrames in training processes. Methods for converting Series to DataFrames for better formatting are explained, alongside automatic index creation and custom indexing. Data types in Pandas are classified, with integers labeled as int64 and non-primitive types as objects. The segment also covers filtering using boolean expressions, creating Series with the PD.Series constructor, and modifying DataFrame indices.

children.title: Pandas Series: Advantages, Indexing, and Selection
children.summary: The advantages of using a Series over a DataFrame in modeling are discussed, emphasizing the need for DataFrames in training processes. Methods for converting Series to DataFrames for better print formatting are explained, along with an overview of Series and index objects, including automatic index creation. Custom indexing is demonstrated with examples, and the classification of data types in Pandas is clarified, noting that integers are labeled as int64 while non-primitive types are objects. Finally, the process of selecting values from a Series using labels and the use of loc and iloc for data extraction are covered.

children.title: Advantages of Series vs DataFrame in Modeling
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the advantages of using a Series object compared to a DataFrame, particularly in the context of modeling. It highlights that many modeling processes require input in the form of a DataFrame for training and optimization.

children.title: Transforming Series to DataFrames and Printing
children.summary: Different methods exist to convert a 1D series into a DataFrame and vice versa, primarily for the purpose of improving print formatting. While Series objects are not displayed in a table-like format, transforming them into DataFrames enhances their visual representation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And there are different ways that we will say that quickly we can transform a 1D series into a data frame and vice versa. But there is no clear other advantage other than like if you're also printing. Serious object is not going to be printed nicely so you're seeing that this is not table like versus if you want it to be table like you might want to turn it into a data frame and use it there. children.title: Custom Indexing in Pandas Series
children.summary: To create a Pandas Series with a custom index, specify the values and their corresponding index labels, such as tagging -1 with 'a', 10 with 'b', and 2 with 'c'. The index can be modified later by resetting it to new labels like 'first', 'second', and 'third', while keeping the original values unchanged. Changes to the index are reflected when accessed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But what if I want a custom index value? If I want to custom index value, what I'll do is I'll say that Get all of these values. This is the constructor for creating series object i'm gonna tell that okay get all of these values negative one ten and two and then i'm also saying that But the index that you want to tag each one of these values with is that for negative one, I want you to tag it with a for 10, I want you to tag it with b and for two, I want you to tag it with c. And that's what I'll see on the right hand side here. So there is a control that if you want to provide your own index, you can provide your own index as well. of all of those labels or the values in the index object. And I can also go ahead and change the index object later on. Say that you created the index object or a serious object with a specific index. You change your mind. You might want to go back and change that value. It's just attribute that you're resetting. So I'm going to say that, okay, this is a stud index. Now I change my mind and go ahead and change the value to be first, second and third. So the index is now changing to first, second and third. And then the values remain the state negative one, ten, two, which is what I had before as well. Again, if I go ahead and get the stud index, the changes are reflected, so I I should be able to have access to what I want it. Video: 1: v

children.title: Pandas data types: integers and objects
children.summary: In pandas, integer values are automatically labeled as int64, but non-primitive types are tagged as objects. If data is not loaded correctly, even numerical values may be classified as objects, necessitating a cast to the appropriate type.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Any questions so far? Questions? Great question. So I forgot to mention that here because my values were integer, it was automatically labeling that as in 64 and it's not integers in 64 bit. And that's how pandas is implemented. But anything other than primitive types, integer float, everything else is going to be tagged as objects and you'll keep seeing object object over and over again. So most of the time, it is not numerical value and it's objects. And a lot of time, if you, for example, don't do the loading of the data properly, it will even load the integer values or full load values as object, then you need to cast that. Video: 1: v

children.title: Selecting Values from Pandas Series
children.summary: The lecture discusses how to select values from a Pandas Series object using labels. It explains that each label acts like a key in a dictionary, allowing the retrieval of corresponding values. An example is provided with a Series containing values and labels A, B, C, and D, demonstrating how to access the value associated with label A.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're going to talk about how we can cast the types in the next couple of lectures. Good question. Let's keep all of moving all of the questions on the slider, because our TAs, we should be able to respond pretty quickly. I have 50 something it's nice to go, exciting. But let's get to it. So if I want to go ahead and make a selection, so we created a serious object, now is the time that we want to use it. And we want to select a number of values in the serious object. So there are three different ways that I can do it. I can say that, okay, I'm going to get access to the element that I want using its label. And I only have a single label that I want to use or extract a single value. You provide that single label, and it should be able to give you that. So the way that we'll do it is that, OK, we created this series object. You can verify that the series object that I have has values negative. 4-0-2-0-6, 4-0-2-0-6, and then it has all of those labeled ABC&D and then ABC&D are all there. So if I want to go ahead and say that, oh, I want to get the value that corresponds to label A. What it will do is it will go ahead and say that, think about labeled as the keys in the dictionary that you'll use them in order to look up the value, right? Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: We're going to talk about how we can cast the types in the next couple of lectures. Good question. Let's keep all of moving all of the questions on the slider, because our TAs, we should be able to respond pretty quickly. I have 50 something it's nice to go, exciting. But let's get to it. So if I want to go ahead and make a selection, so we created a serious object, now is the time that we want to use it. And we want to select a number of values in the serious object. So there are three different ways that I can do it. I can say that, okay, I'm going to get access to the element that I want using its label. And I only have a single label that I want to use or extract a single value. You provide that single label, and it should be able to give you that. So the way that we'll do it is that, OK, we created this series object. You can verify that the series object that I have has values negative. 4-0-2-0-6, 4-0-2-0-6, and then it has all of those labeled ABC&D and then ABC&D are all there. So if I want to go ahead and say that, oh, I want to get the value that corresponds to label A. children.title: Extracting Values with Corresponding Labels in Pandas
children.summary: When extracting values from a Pandas Series using a list of labels, it's important to return both the values and their corresponding labels to maintain clarity. This ensures that the values can be accurately associated with their respective keys, preventing confusion about which value corresponds to which label. The resulting data structure from this operation will be a Series, which contains both labels and values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's a list of labels. So with a list of labels here, I would say that, okay, I want the values that correspond to A and C. What are those values? So let's take a look. A should give me four, and then C should give me zero. So there's one way that we could say that, okay, give four and zero and whatever order that you want. But then later on, it will be hard for us to figure out whether four was corresponding to the key C or four was corresponding to key A. And that's why every time that we have a list of labels that we're extracting the value for, we should return also the corresponding labels as well so that it's distinguishable, that what is for corresponding to and what is zero corresponding to and we can make a distinction between them. So therefore, In that specific case, what we'll do is we'll pass this entire set of values. And for me to pass the labels together with the values... What is that data structure look like? That is a serious object, right? So a data type that has labels and it has values and then the resulting data type is going to be serious. Video: 1: v

children.title: Series Object Output Type Explanation
children.summary: The output type is a Series object to differentiate values corresponding to various keys or labels, allowing for indexed access. When only a single label is used, the output type corresponds to the data type of that value, such as integer. Additionally, a condition can be applied to extract values, such as those greater than zero, without manually inspecting each label.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What I want you when initially in the lecture I said that I want you to make the logic for yourself about why things happen in a certain way this is one of them. So why was the output type a serious object? This is because I needed to distinguish between the values that correspond to those different, different keys or different labels. So that's why the return type was a serious object. And the serious data structure was the only thing that could basically perform exactly what I wanted. Have indexed, have the value so I can index further into it. But when I had only one single label, single label, you just have the values. So you're going to get the data type to be the data type of that value, which was integer. So the last one is a little bit more complicated. So the last one is that we're saying that instead of us providing a list of labels, what I want to do is I want to say that I have a condition. My condition is that I want to, for example, extract all of the values that are greater than zero. But I don't want to go ahead and manually inspect and then extract the labels and provide you with the labels. Video: 1: v

children.title: Filtering and Creating Pandas Series
children.summary: Filtering in Pandas involves using boolean expressions to include or exclude rows based on conditions. Boolean indexing allows extraction of values from a Series that meet specified criteria, such as values greater than zero. Series objects can be created using the PD.Series constructor, with options for default or custom indexing. Additionally, the index of a DataFrame can be modified by assigning a new list of labels.

children.title: Filtering Data in Pandas Series
children.summary: Filtering in Pandas involves creating a boolean expression to assign true or false values to rows. Rows marked as true will be included in the output, while those marked as false will be excluded.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The way that we can do that is by... a filtering condition or that we will write a bully an expression that will tag true false values to each one of those rows and then we'll use those true false values in order to filter the values in the uh in the series object that we had any road that corresponds to true is going to be uh participating in the output any road that is false means that it doesn't meet the condition and then it's not going to be in the output. children.title: Boolean indexing in Pandas Series
children.summary: To extract values greater than zero from a Pandas Series, first create a Boolean condition using the expression 'S > 0'. This condition generates a series of true/false values, which can then be used to index the original Series, returning only the values that meet the condition.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: it's a two step process. The first step as I said is that we'll write a Boolean condition or a Boolean expression that will create all of those true false values that correspond to each one of those rows. And then what we'll do is we'll use those true false values in order to index into the series object. That's a lot of words. Let's see how that works in practice. My condition is that I want to extract all of the values that are greater than zero. How do I do it? First step, write the Boolean condition. My Boolean condition is that I'm going to say that I want all of the S values that are greater than zero. Right, simple as that. When we say, hand does runs everything in a vectorized operation, that's what we meant. Right? We say S greater than 0, it knows that it's a vector, it will go ahead and apply it to all of the rows, without me explicitly looping over all of the values. That's the beauty of doing everything in serious object or in data free. So I have the labels I have true false conditions. So what happens next? I can use these true false conditions or these values in order to index into the actual thing. By indexing into actual thing, but I mean is that I'll go ahead and say that, okay, A, the corresponding values equal to true. So, A is going to pass, is going to exist in the output. And you can verify that the value is greater and zero, so obviously everything checks out. So D is also saying that the corresponding values true, and we can verify it, it's greater than zero. leaf_chunk.audio: it's a two step process. The first step as I said is that we'll write a Boolean condition or a Boolean expression that will create all of those true false values that correspond to each one of those rows. And then what we'll do is we'll use those true false values in order to index into the series object. That's a lot of words. Let's see how that works in practice. My condition is that I want to extract all of the values that are greater than zero. How do I do it? First step, write the Boolean condition. My Boolean condition is that I'm going to say that I want all of the S values that are greater than zero. Right, simple as that. When we say, hand does runs everything in a vectorized operation, that's what we meant. Right? We say S greater than 0, it knows that it's a vector, it will go ahead and apply it to all of the rows, without me explicitly looping over all of the values. That's the beauty of doing everything in serious object or in data free. So I have the labels I have true false conditions. So what happens next? I can use these true false conditions or these values in order to index into the actual thing. By indexing into actual thing, but I mean is that I'll go ahead and say that, okay, A, the corresponding values equal to true. So, A is going to pass, is going to exist in the output. And you can verify that the value is greater and zero, so obviously everything checks out. So D is also saying that the corresponding values true, and we can verify it, it's greater than zero. So these are the two rows that are going to, or two values that are going to be in the output.

children.title: Creating Series Objects in Pandas
children.summary: To create a Series object in Pandas, import pandas as PD and use the PD.Series constructor with a list of values. The output will automatically assign labels to the values starting from 0, and attributes can be accessed using the 'values' and 'index' attributes. Custom indexing can also be implemented by specifying an index during the creation of the Series.

children.title: Pandas Series: Selection and Filtering
children.summary: A Pandas Series object is created with index values A, B, C, and D. Single label selection retrieves corresponding values, while filtering can be applied using conditions such as checking for even numbers. A Boolean expression is used to filter the Series, confirming the identification of even and odd numbers.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that's how we can do it. Here, kind of summarizing everything that we've seen in the selection, right? We create a series objects. So here, the series object is created by all of these values. Index values are ABC and D. Taking a look, it looks something like this. You remember that we could do selection by only a single label. So if I use a single label, I will be able to get only the value corresponding to that label. So you take a look at the corresponding value 6. So I got 6. If I go ahead and request for CND, you're seeing that I'm expecting to get the filtered version of this series object with the label CND and the values 1 and 6. So that's what's happening here as well. So I want to try a different filtering condition. So here I would say that instead of filtering the values based on the values being greater than zero, I want to filter values that are even, right? I want to check whether the remainder of each one of those values by two is equal to zero or not. So remember that there was a two-step process. The first step was to write the Boolean expression. So I'm going to just write a Boolean expression. No, that's... Pan does under the hood is going to apply it in a vectorized way to all of the rows so I don't need to go ahead and write a loop so here I would say that So the name was s, right? So the remainder of values by 2 should be equal to 0 for all of those values to be to be even right and then the result i'll take you like it says that the first one and last one are even and then the middle two are odd numbers uh kind of confirming that even numbers and then odd numbers in the middle which makes sense so what i'll do next is i'll say that okay i'm going to index into s using this condition even that I created. and then take a look. was able to like filter that for me. children.title: Pandas DataFrame and Series Creation Techniques
children.summary: The segment covers the creation of Series and DataFrames in Pandas, emphasizing the importance of alignment and filtering based on conditions. It details methods for constructing DataFrames from CSV files, dictionaries, and Series, highlighting the use of indices for data organization. Additionally, it discusses modifying DataFrame indices and the automatic labeling of columns when converting Series to DataFrames.

children.title: Pandas Series and DataFrame Creation Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses serious objects in Pandas, confirming understanding through engagement. It covers constructing a Series object using values and index labels, filtering a DataFrame based on conditions, and the process of creating DataFrames from multiple Series objects with a shared index, emphasizing the importance of alignment in data structure.

children.title: Understanding Serious Objects in Pandas
children.summary: The segment focuses on confirming the understanding of serious objects in Pandas, specifically referencing a question related to the Kira Cut. The instructor encourages engagement by asking if students want to lock in their understanding.

children.title: Creating a Pandas Series Object
children.summary: A Series object is constructed using two sets of values: the first set, 'four, five, and six', represents the actual values, while the second set, 'one, two, and three', serves as the index labels. The discussion emphasizes understanding the distinction between these two components in the Series creation process.

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series Objects
children.summary: To create a DataFrame, combine multiple Series objects, each with a shared index. The index values, which range from 0 to 181, allow for the alignment of data across columns. This results in a 2D structure where all Series share the same index.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So then the next thing that we want to talk about is how can we combine all of these series objects in order to create a data frame. So in order to create a data frame, you think about having a series object with the index, those index values that we had in the election, they were automatically created, they're starting from 0 all the way to 181. And then the name of the first column or the first series was here, I'll keep combining with the candidate series, I'll keep combining it with all of the other columns that we have all the way to the point that we create this 2D structure. What I want you to notice is that each one of these series object, each one of them actually had... And index right and these index values were the same or this index object or the values in that index object were shared between all of those series objects and that's why we could combine them together after we combine all of those columns together now we have that same index. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: So then the next thing that we want to talk about is how can we combine all of these series objects in order to create a data frame. So in order to create a data frame, you think about having a series object with the index, those index values that we had in the election, they were automatically created, they're starting from 0 all the way to 181. And then the name of the first column or the first series was here, I'll keep combining with the candidate series, I'll keep combining it with all of the other columns that we have all the way to the point that we create this 2D structure. What I want you to notice is that each one of these series object, each one of them actually had... children.title: DataFrame structure: labels and creation
children.summary: A DataFrame in Pandas is a 2D structure characterized by two sets of labels: one for columns and one for rows. Each column is identified by a name that serves as its label, facilitating access to individual elements. The segment concludes with a transition to the creation of a DataFrame.

children.title: DataFrame structure: labels and creation
children.summary: A DataFrame in Pandas is a 2D structure with two sets of labels: one for columns and one for rows. Each column has a name that serves as its label, allowing access to individual elements. The segment concludes with a transition to creating a DataFrame.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: refer to as the index of this entire data frame so that we can distinguish between different rows using those labels. And then what you're seeing is that for each one of these columns that we had, we had the name. So the name is going to appear as the column label. So the first series object had the name year or the name of that series object was year. So the label for the first column is going to be here. the label, the name for the second column is candidate, so the label for the second column is going to be candidate, so on and so forth. And that's how data frame is created. So again, think about data frame as this 2D structure. It has two sets of labels. It has labels for the columns. It has labels for the rows, and then we'll work with those labels in order to get access to individual elements. So with that simple explanation and now I want to go ahead and create data frame. children.title: Creating DataFrames: Methods and Indexing
children.summary: DataFrames can be created from various sources, primarily CSV files, using the read_csv function. The DataFrame constructor accepts 'data', 'index', and 'columns', with 'index' defaulting to a range if not specified. Setting 'year' as the index allows it to serve as a label, while using lists with specified column names enhances clarity. For multiple columns, two-dimensional lists can be used, where the outer list represents rows and inner lists represent columns.

children.title: Creating DataFrames: Syntax and Parameters
children.summary: DataFrames can be created in various ways, but typically, users will load data from CSV files or spreadsheets rather than creating them manually. The DataFrame constructor accepts several arguments: 'data', 'index', and 'columns'. The 'index' is optional and defaults to a range starting from zero if not provided, while 'columns' allows for custom labels to enhance readability.

It also has a bunch of arguments that we can pass. The arguments that we pass, the first one is data exactly similar to series. The second one is index. children.title: Creating DataFrames from CSV files
children.summary: DataFrames can be created using CSV files, primarily through the function read_csv. The path to the CSV file must be provided, and upon execution, the data will be loaded into a DataFrame. Additionally, an optional index can be specified to use one of the columns as the index instead of automatic numbering.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The most useful one and the one that you will see most is going to be using a CSV file. But there are also different manual ways of creating a data frame. How can we create a data from using CSV file? There is a function called the read underscore CSV. And you should have your data handy. And then you should pass the path to the data to this read underscore CSV. So here my data is in data folder in elections underscore CSV. So we can verify that. You also have access to that. There is a data folder. There is elections.csv that we should be able to read the data. And then automatically all the data is going to be uploaded here. Right? And you have a data frame. Nice and ready to go. Elections is of type now data frame. I could also provide index. Remember that the first argument was data, the second argument was index, that was optional. But you could say that, okay, of all of the columns that I have, I want to use one of them as an index, as opposed to automatically numbering each one of the rows. Video: 1: v

children.title: Indexing in Pandas: Year as Label
children.summary: The index column is set to 'year', which is indicated by its bold formatting on the left side of the DataFrame. This signifies that 'year' serves as a label and an index for the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in this case, we're saying that the index column is equal to year. And then that's why the year right now is moved to the left, right? And all of those year values are bolded. So having a bold value to the left of the data firm at the top of the data firm should tell you that they're labels. And now year is a label. Year is an index. Video: 1: v

children.title: Creating DataFrames with Column Names
children.summary: DataFrames can be created using lists with specified column names, which improves clarity over unnamed columns. For example, initializing a DataFrame with values 1, 2, and 3 and naming the column 'numbers' results in a structured table format, contrasting with the series format.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There are different ways that we can set index, reset index. So let's move year back to the data frame. If we want to use it later on, and we're going to see that next. you So. The second part or the second way of creating a data frame is using a list with column names if you want to provide column names as well. So for example, if I provide this is very similar to what we had as a series, I would say that okay, I'm creating a data frame object calling the constructor and then I'll say that the values that I want to store is one, two and three. So we did exactly the same thing with series object. This time I'm saying that go ahead and name this column as numbers so that it's not like blank. If you don't name it, it will give you the name zero. So column zero does not have any meaning. You just want to have a description of what attribute you're restoring in that column. So that's why most of the time we would provide a thing for a mission about columns. So I'm saying that columns is equal to numbers and then we move on. So this is a one column data frame, right? You see how it's nicely structured as opposed to if this was a series object, if I was going to print it, it would be like in that veer shape format. But here you're seeing that in kind of a table format. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: There are different ways that we can set index, reset index. So let's move year back to the data frame. If we want to use it later on, and we're going to see that next. you So. The second part or the second way of creating a data frame is using a list with column names if you want to provide column names as well. So for example, if I provide this is very similar to what we had as a series, I would say that okay, I'm creating a data frame object calling the constructor and then I'll say that the values that I want to store is one, two and three. So we did exactly the same thing with series object. This time I'm saying that go ahead and name this column as numbers so that it's not like blank. If you don't name it, it will give you the name zero. So column zero does not have any meaning. You just want to have a description of what attribute you're restoring in that column. So that's why most of the time we would provide a thing for a mission about columns. So I'm saying that columns is equal to numbers and then we move on. So this is a one column data frame, right? You see how it's nicely structured as opposed to if this was a series object, if I was going to print it, it would be like in that veer shape format. But here you're seeing that in kind of a table format.

children.title: Two-dimensional lists for multiple columns
children.summary: To handle multiple columns, transition from one-dimensional to two-dimensional lists, where the outer list represents rows and inner lists represent columns. Each inner list corresponds to a row, and as more columns are added, the structure remains 2D with the outer list containing inner lists for each row.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What if I have multiple columns? If I have multiple columns, I need to move beyond a one-dimensional list and go into two-dimensional lists. So you think about what the list that we had here, right? So the list that I had here, each this outer list, was basically going over different rows. Each one of those elements, they were referring to different rows that I had. Here is also the same thing. The outer kind of list is going over rows and the inner list, which I have here, is each one of those Actually the... The other way around. So that was the first column and then here I have um each one of those rows. So you're referring to if you're taking a look at one and one, here is one row, and then here this is also row two, and then what you're seeing is here that I'm collecting, all of them stacking them over into that column. And exactly that's what's happening here is that these ones are being stacked. in one column. So that's kind of the structure if you want to keep adding columns. What's happening is that you will still have a 2D structure list. And each one of those inner inner lists are going to be referring to that entire road that you had. So the number of elements that you will put in the inner list is going to increase. But then the structure is still going to be this 2D structure of having one inner list and one outer list as well. So here what I'm saying is that because each one of these in your list have the dimension of two, I have two columns and therefore I'm providing the name for them that the columns are now number. leaf_chunk.audio: What if I have multiple columns? If I have multiple columns, I need to move beyond a one-dimensional list and go into two-dimensional lists. So you think about what the list that we had here, right? So the list that I had here, each this outer list, was basically going over different rows. Each one of those elements, they were referring to different rows that I had. Here is also the same thing. The outer kind of list is going over rows and the inner list, which I have here, is each one of those Actually the... The other way around. So that was the first column and then here I have um each one of those rows. So you're referring to if you're taking a look at one and one, here is one row, and then here this is also row two, and then what you're seeing is here that I'm collecting, all of them stacking them over into that column. And exactly that's what's happening here is that these ones are being stacked. in one column. So that's kind of the structure if you want to keep adding columns. What's happening is that you will still have a 2D structure list. And each one of those inner inner lists are going to be referring to that entire road that you had. So the number of elements that you will put in the inner list is going to increase. But then the structure is still going to be this 2D structure of having one inner list and one outer list as well. So here what I'm saying is that because each one of these in your list have the dimension of two, I have two columns and therefore I'm providing the name for them that the columns are now number.

children.title: Creating DataFrames: Dictionaries and Series
children.summary: DataFrames can be created using dictionaries where column names are keys and values are lists, facilitating easy column reference. They can also be formed from Series objects, which must have matching indices for proper alignment, and can be converted using the DataFrame constructor or the 'to_frame' method. This understanding aids in data manipulation and error resolution.

children.title: Creating DataFrames with Dictionaries
children.summary: DataFrames can be created using a dictionary structure where column names are keys and their corresponding values are lists. This method allows for easy reference to columns in a key-value format. Additionally, when iterating over rows, specific values for each column can be assigned by referencing the respective keys.

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series Objects
children.summary: DataFrames can be created using series, which are key-value pairs where keys represent column names and values are series objects. An example is provided where a two-column DataFrame is formed using a dictionary, with each column corresponding to a series object, illustrating the association of values and indices.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, the dictionary is out of the way. The second most common way of creating a data frame is using series because naturally we talked about data frame as being a collection of series. Being more accurate by collection, I mean a key value pair. So we have keys being each one of those column names and then values being a series object. So what I can do here is that I can say that, oh, I want a two column data frame. So one column has a name A column, one column has a name B column. So I'm going to go ahead and create a data frame using a dictionary, right? And my dictionary has two columns and two keys. But what are the values? The values that you're seeing here is each one of them is a serious object. So this is a serious object which is created right here, which we know how to create it. Right, and then the values that are associated with B column are also coming from this s underscore B series object. You can see that the values are B1B2B3 and the index is R1R2R3. Video: 1: v

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series with Matching Indices
children.summary: To create a DataFrame from multiple Series, they must have matching indices, which allows for proper alignment in the DataFrame. A Series can be converted into a DataFrame using the DataFrame constructor or by calling the Series' method 'to_frame'. Understanding Series as collections of key-value pairs aids in data manipulation and error resolution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The trick here is that if you're Using multiple series in order to combine them in order to create a data frame they need to have matching index and that's why the matching index right here is actually R1 R2R3 they both have R1 R2R3 and that's what's going to show up here R1 R2R3 Cool. Okay, so now everything about labels how series was created and how data frame is created and how we can think about a series using a collect a data frame as a collection of series and then how everything is key value pair and that's how you can think about key value pair in the colons key value pair in the rose is going to. Help you in order to make sense of any error that you're getting or any time that you want to load the data filter data you have everything that you need. Tiny bit at the bottom here. You're seeing that if I have a series object and I want to turn it into a data frame, I can just directly pass it to the data frame constructor and it will turn it into a series object for me or I can directly say series dot two frame. children.title: Creating and Modifying DataFrame Indexes in Pandas
children.summary: A function can convert a Series object into a DataFrame with automatic column labeling. Various index types, including non-numeric indices, can be created, and indexing allows for data extraction even with non-unique indices. The .set_index method modifies the index, while .reset_index maintains order and creates automatic indices. Unique column names are crucial for clarity, and DataFrames have attributes like index, columns, and shape to describe their structure.

children.title: Creating DataFrames from Series Objects
children.summary: A function is used to convert a Series object into a DataFrame, which can lead to automatic column labeling starting from zero if column names are not provided. This results in the first column being labeled as zero, and subsequent columns being labeled sequentially.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's a function that we have in order to turn a series object into a data frame. Right, so that's one of the questions that was asked about how we can turn it into Turn one to another. children.title: Indexing in DataFrames: Unique and Non-unique Indices
children.summary: DataFrames allow indexing to extract specific data, such as counting occurrences of a value. The index does not need to be unique; for example, numeric indices can repeat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is an easy way that if you want to index into a data frame and get all the times that for example is sort of in candidate one friend for presidency and then count how many times it was you can directly use that using this key value kind of structure that we have. One more thing is that the index does not necessarily need to be unique. So in this specific case, in the left-hand side, you're seeing that all of the index values are starting from zero all the way to whatever, and they're numeric, they're unique. Video: 1: v

One more thing is that the index does not necessarily need to be unique. So in this specific case, in the left-hand side, you're seeing that all of the index values are starting from zero all the way to whatever, and they're numeric, they're unique.
